There are two possible issues that can be caused by this that come to my mind. The first is that if someone would access the next_identifier ivar inside the for loop, it would contain a wrong value, which can help us, but the value won't actually be a next identifier. The second is that we would be relying on the code to always go thru all the list, because if it would break somewhere, the next_identifier ivar would contain a wrong value.

Suggestion for changes:
1. Change: threads => client threads, i.e.  if you want to have multiple client threads interacting with a single MXNet model
2. Add this: 
For example, if you use MXNet from a gunicorn HTTP server, set the MXNET_ENGINE_TYPE to NaiveEngine. The gunicorn HTTP server use a pre-fork worker model, i.e. it (pre) forks and keeps a group of processes running to handle various incoming HTTP requests and then forks additional processes if the load goes up. Since the fork of a process replicates the complete process address space including threads, keeping MXNet single-threaded via the use of NaiveEngine makes it safe.

- There is always the trade-off between simplicity of interfaces and simplicity of internal implementation. I want to keep the outside interface as simple and understandable by only returning one memory requirement instead of bothering the user to pass around "magic" values between functions. The downside is that the internal implementation is a bit more cumbersome. As workspace queries are cheap,  there is no effective downside on performance as well (note that this is anyway only an issue on CPU). 

- You can't just create an int-variable as this must be device memory. 

- Whether a function succeeds is checked/handled bu CUSOLVER_CALL macro. The additional "info" field just indicates specific rows/calls that may have problems in case of an error. We ignore this value in all lapack calls also on CPU.

@jni I chose this design because it encourages reusing the existing gabor kernel. As of right now, the `filters.gabor` function creates a new gabor kernel every time you use it. This becomes very expensive if I, say, want to apply the same gabor filter to 1000 images.

While there is already a method of eliminating this expense by creating a gabor kernel and applying it to the images yourself, I would posit that most users would not consider this as they would immediately gravitate towards the `gabor` function, which is not obviously expensive from the documentation when applied many times. Furthermore, if one wanted to reproduce what the `gabor` function did, they would have to look at the source code and figure out exactly how we were able to apply the filter to an image.

Although I generally prefer functional design to object-oriented design, I think that this choice is justified for the following reasons:
- NumPy is already object-oriented. If we're representing the kernel as an `np.ndarray` anyways, it would be most useful to extend this interface to apply it as well.
- This tags along the filter function to the kernel instead of having to use one function to make the kernel and another to apply it (this would be the functional alternative). This is also a lot more intuitive.

This won't work if a user runs Tox locally where the `CHANGE_TARGET` environment variable is not defined or on Jenkins when testing a branch that is not a pull request (e.g. `master`) where this environment variable is not defined.

Since Tox doesn't support conditional statements I would use its ability to create generative testing environments and define `-partial` testing environments:

```ini
envlist =
    py{27,34,35}{,-partial},
    docs,
    linters,
    packaging,
    extra,
    migrations
```

and then use this here to only conditionally include the added arguments:

```ini
# run tests
    python tests/manage.py test {env:TEST_SUITE:resolwe_bio} --noinput \
        # only test changes against a given Git commit reference for *-partial
        # testing environments (default to "master" branch)
        py{27,34,35}-partial: --only-changes-to {env:RESOLWE_TEST_ONLY_CHANGES_TO:master} \
        py{27,34,35}-partial: --changes-file-types .resolwebio-filetypes.yml \
        --verbosity 2 --parallel
```

The `Jenkinsfile` would need to be updated to handle this:
```
if (env.CHANGE_TARGET) {
    // run partial test suite depending on detected changes to the target
    // branch if we are testing a pull request
    withEnv(["RESOLWE_TEST_ONLY_CHANGES_TO=origin/${env.CHANGE_TARGET}"]) {
        sh "tox -e py34-partial"
    }
} else {
    sh "tox -e py34"
}
```

It would be nice if you also updated the documentation on [Using Tox](http://resolwe-bio.readthedocs.io/en/stable/contributing.html#using-tox) and mention the `-partial` version of test environments and how a developer can control the Git commit against which changes are calculated with `RESOLWE_TEST_ONLY_CHANGES_TO` environment variable.

So for instance:

```
from kolibri.utils import conf

DATABASES = conf.global_settings.DATABASES
```

Another thing: `conf.global_settings` can be made a completely read-only instance after instantiating to guarantee against wrong use.

A friend of mine just tipped me that even for rigorous cases, they would replace `os.environ` with a read-only dict. This method offers a second approach to users for acting on the fact their message has timed out. If, for example, a message is timed out and about to undergo an expensive operation; the user can check `is_alive` and simply discard the message. 

The alternative using callbacks would require some significant overhead: either a registry of messages that have timed out so the same thing can be accomplished, or setting a similar flag in the callback itself. Imho neither of those two are ideal, so offering this interface gives the user a simple alternative.

I have a couple of questions.

1. I understand the desire to use an existing library for concurrency. If we get to the point where we need complex or robust concurrency handling, I agree that it would be useful. However, the decorator is only 4 lines of code and for now I think that'll be sufficient. Do we want to add another dependency just to replace the decorator?

2. We could move the decorator to a utils module. I put it in this class originally because it references the internal `_lock` attribute of the class. This is an implicit dependency for using this locking decorator; it will only work on `KmipClient` methods, or any other class that defines it's own lock. (As an aside, `oslo.concurrency.lockutils` uses a lock and semaphore pool instead, where you name your locks and specify them explicitly when using the decorator. Useful if your critical sections are accessed at various points in your architecture, maybe a little overkill here.)

First, Ke's characterization of data compat vs. code compat is spot on. They are two different things, and the vast majority of protobuf versioning rules are focused on the former and IMO ignore the latter.  If you read the [protobuf versioning rules](https://developers.google.com/protocol-buffers/docs/proto3#updating), it say it's totally OK to switch from int32 to bool, but that would break A TON of code if one were to regenerate their language bindings using protoc against the new .proto file. 

As for Ke's proposed solution, while we may elect to provide a shared library, the specification cannot mandate or really assume it will be used everywhere. We have to assume a reasonable number of people will just run protoc on the latest .proto file and have to absorb our "data compat/code incompat) changes (assuming we every let them happen).  BTW, we are some of those people so we get to live with whatever policy we apply. 

As for @dzhulgakov's suggestion of keeping a field type immutable, I'm OK with that PROVIDED we use a new name for the field. Otherwise, we get the worst of both worlds: we cause the same breaking change to consumers of the .proto file AND we add versioning complexity to consumers as the field's value can now appear in two different places based on version.

BTW, once we hit V1.0.0, I want to adopt @dzhulgakov's approach. Leading up to that, I'm more liberal, as the blast radius of a BC is still fairly contained.  That honeymoon will end soon enough, so maybe we just adopt that policy now (with my addendum of not reusing the same field NAME as well as field NUMBER).
